front: Concept: pigments are mixed into binders such as egg yolk in this painting medium
Concept: Cennino Cennini's Il libro dell'arte (The Craftsman's Handbook) was the first extensive European text to discuss this painting medium and provided ratios for mixing it
Concept: this painting medium was fully supplanted by oil painting during the Renaissance (around 1500), having been the dominant panel-painting medium of medieval Europe
Concept: gesso, a mixture of plaster and rabbit-skin glue, was first developed as a ground for this painting medium
Concept: must be applied in thin layers to prevent cracking; creates a translucent, matte appearance
Concept: this medium's 20th-century revival involved Paul Cadmus and Jacob Lawrence; Andrew Wyeth learned it from Peter Hurd and used it for Christina's World
Concept: the Birmingham Group learned this technique from a handbook translated by Edwardian artist Christiana Herringham
Concept: the Fayum mummy portraits combined this painting medium with encaustic
Painting: Duccio's medieval panel paintings, including the Rucellai Madonna and the Maesta, are canonical examples of this medium combined with gold leaf in the Byzantine icon tradition
Painting: the Rucellai Madonna (1285, Siena) uses this medium and gold leaf to depict three angels on either side of the Virgin and Child
Painting: a primary reason for this work's deterioration is Leonardo's innovative but unstable mixture of oil paint with tempera, applied as fresco-secco with a white-lead undercoat
Painting: depicting a giantess enshrouded in golden wheat circled by geese, this Leonora Carrington painting was made in egg tempera
back: The Giantess (The Guardian of the Egg) (Leonora Carrington)

front: Painting: in this Leonora Carrington tempera work, a white figure emerges from an orange tree
back: The Kitchen Garden on the Eyot (Leonora Carrington)

front: Painting: Leonora Carrington wrote that the egg was 'the dividing line between the Big and the Small' during her stay in a sanatorium during this conflict, which contextualizes her tempera paintings
